docs.title: Home
docs.text:
$ grep "snippets" ~/s-shifat/projects/
./automation/quick_fix.sh
./utils/parse_data.py
./scripts/dev_shortcuts.sh
./git/alias_config.sh
./latex/templates/report.tex
./snippets/
Hi, You've landed on my code dumping place. It exists because of these recurring thoughts:

"Oh! this thing again, and I forgot how I resolved it."
"I'll probably need this thing later."

Whether it’s data science, environment fixes, or system tweaks, whenever I figure something out that might save me alot-of-google-searching in the future, I drop it here.

💻 What’s inside?

  • Data Science Stuff (mostly .py)
  • Automation, utility scripts, environment setups (.sh, .py)
  • Git configurations & aliases (.sh, .gitconfig)
  • LaTeX templates for reports & papers (.tex)
  • Code snippets for quick reference (.md, .py)
  • and some other random stuff.

If you find something useful, feel free to grab it.

Happy coding! 🚀

docs.title: Set a separate ssh key for one or more repo
docs.text:
While adding your machine to github via ssh, we followed github's guide line

However, we can also set up one or more ssh keys for one or more repos.

This can be handy in different scenarios. For example, say for the default setup I have generated ssh key with a passkey. So, for every push/pull action I have to authenticate using that passkey. Now I have a specific repository where I don't want this behavior, rather don't use any passkey at all. To achieve this we can do this:

Run:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_newID -N ""
  • This creates a new key (id_newID) with no passphrase.
  • The private key is stored in ~/.ssh/id_newID.
  • The public key is stored in ~/.ssh/id_newID.pub.

Now, to push / pull do:

GIT_SSH_COMMAND="ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_newID" git pull

To parsist this behavior, in the repo, do: 

git config core.sshCommand "ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_newID"

docs.title: Add Pdf Pages to a document
docs.text:
To include pages from a pdf to a document you can do:

\usepackage{pdfpages} % To include PDF pages

\begin{document}

...

\includepdf[pages=-]{yourfile.pdf} % The `pages=-` option includes all pages from the PDF


\end{document}

docs.title: add_fonts
docs.text:
# A pseudo scenario
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(0, 100, 0.2)
y = x**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.plot(x, y)

docs.title: Option1: Fonts installed and Setup in global scope
docs.text:
If a particular font is already installed in the development machine/system and for the plotting you want to use it in each plot (ie. global scope)

plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'Times New Roman'

docs.title: Option2: Fonts installed and Setup Plot Element specific
docs.text:
Tip: Most of the text contatining elements have font or similar keyword argument 

ax.set_title('x vs y', font='Times New Roman')  # Title
ax.set_xlabel('x m/s', font='Arial')  # Label
ax.set_xticks(
    np.arange(0, 100, 20),
    lables=np.arange(0, 100, 20),
    font='Source Code Pro'
)  # tick labels

docs.title: List The font names available to matplotlib
Load a font file without systemwide installation
docs.text:
from matplotlib import font_manager

font_path = "path/to/font.ttf"  
font_props = font_manager.FontProperties(fname=font_path)

ax.plot(x, y)

# Apply the custom font
ax.set_title("x vs y", fontproperties=font_props, fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel("X-axis Label", fontproperties=font_props)
ax.set_ylabel("Y-axis Label", fontproperties=font_props)
ax.legend(prop=font_props)

docs.title: Setup Font Size
docs.text:
setup font size of different components in global scope. More at: Customizing Matplotlib with Style sheets and rcParams

import matplotlib as mpl

# Set LaTeX-style font
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "text.usetex": True,
    "font.family": "serif",
    "font.serif": ["Computer Modern"],
    "axes.labelsize": 12,
    "axes.titlesize": 14,
    "legend.fontsize": 12,
    "xtick.labelsize": 12,
    "ytick.labelsize": 12,
})

docs.title: Change Backend / Use figure toolbars
docs.text:
Use matplotlib figure toolbars in jupyter notebook environment.

matplotlib.use('nbagg')  # For Figure Tool bars embedded in jupyter output cell
Other backends:

matplotlib.use('TkAgg')
# or
matplotlib.use('Qt5Agg')
There are also few other cool backends built-in, which can be divided into 2 categories: Interactive and Non-interactive.

To see what's available:

from matplotlib.backends import backend_registry, BackendFilter
print("Interactives:", backend_registry.list_builtin(BackendFilter.INTERACTIVE))
print("Non-interactives:", backend_registry.list_builtin(BackendFilter.NON_INTERACTIVE))

docs.title: Inline + Interactive
docs.text:
While using ipython and a terminal that supports image rendering like kitty magic commands also work:

Technically this should work in jupyter notebooks too. 

%matplotlib tk
#or
%matplotlib qt

docs.title: Inline + Non-interactive
docs.text:
I am currently using kitty terminal. To use kitty as backend for python's matplotlib library. use the following:

  1. Install it:

    pip install --user matplotlib-backend-kitty

  2. Use it (say in ipython or vanila python): 

    import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('module://matplotlib-backend-kitty')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    . More on this link.

docs.title: Use Latex in global scope
docs.text:
It will display numbers in latex format and also the formula.

plt.rcParams['text.usetex'] = True

docs.title: Format Dates in axis
docs.text:
Let's say I only want to show hours and minutes in the time axis (x-axis)

from matplotlib.dates import DateFormatter

time_format = DateFormatter("%H:%M")

ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(time_format)

python date string formats (I like to call it python date format table) are found here

docs.title: Most frequent mpl snippets
docs.text:
In [1]: Copied! 
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Some nice styling
plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8")
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "Times New Roman",
    "axes.titlesize": 18,
    "axes.labelsize": 14,
    "xtick.labelsize": 12,
    "ytick.labelsize": 12,
})
import numpy as np import matplotlib as mpl import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Some nice styling plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8") mpl.rcParams.update({ "font.family": "Times New Roman", "axes.titlesize": 18, "axes.labelsize": 14, "xtick.labelsize": 12, "ytick.labelsize": 12, }) In [2]: Copied! 
x = np.arange(-4*np.pi, 4*np.pi + 0.01, 0.01)
y = x**3 * np.sin(x) * np.cos(x)

x_left, x_right, interval = -2*np.pi, 2*np.pi, np.deg2rad(90)
custom_xticks = np.arange(x_left, x_right + interval, interval)
custom_xticks_labels = np.int16(np.rint(np.degrees(custom_xticks)))
x = np.arange(-4*np.pi, 4*np.pi + 0.01, 0.01) y = x**3 * np.sin(x) * np.cos(x) x_left, x_right, interval = -2*np.pi, 2*np.pi, np.deg2rad(90) custom_xticks = np.arange(x_left, x_right + interval, interval) custom_xticks_labels = np.int16(np.rint(np.degrees(custom_xticks))) In [3]: Copied! 
fig_size = (8.5, 6)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=fig_size)

ax.plot(x, y)

ax.set(
    title= 'My Title',

    xlabel = '$\\leftarrow X \\rightarrow$',
    xlim = (x_left, x_right),
    xscale = 'linear', # or 'log', <function> etc.

    ylabel = '$\\leftarrow Y \\rightarrow$',
    ylim = (-100, 50),
)


fig.set_layout_engine('constrained')
# fig.savefig('myplot.png', dpi=600)

plt.show()
fig_size = (8.5, 6) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=fig_size) ax.plot(x, y) ax.set( title= 'My Title', xlabel = '$\\leftarrow X \\rightarrow$', xlim = (x_left, x_right), xscale = 'linear', # or 'log', etc. ylabel = '$\\leftarrow Y \\rightarrow$', ylim = (-100, 50), ) fig.set_layout_engine('constrained') # fig.savefig('myplot.png', dpi=600) plt.show()

or use a dictionary with the valid key word arguments $\rightarrow$

ax_properties = {
    "title": "My title",
    "xlabel": "x",
    "ylabel": "y",
    # etc...
}
ax.set(**ax_properties)
In [7]: Copied! 
plt.plot(x, y)

# simplest
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

# or 
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45)
plt.plot(x, y) # simplest plt.xticks(rotation=45) # or ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45) In [8]: Copied! 
plt.plot(x, y)
ax  = plt.gca()

ax.set_xticks(custom_xticks, custom_xticks_labels, rotation=90)

plt.show()
plt.plot(x, y) ax = plt.gca() ax.set_xticks(custom_xticks, custom_xticks_labels, rotation=90) plt.show()
